title: Reducing shower water use without reducing shower quality
subtitle: Aerated showerheads, flow restrictors, and shower timers compared
excerpt: A standard shower head flows at 10-15 litres per minute. An aerated eco showerhead flows at 6-8 litres per minute while producing a similar perceived pressure by mixing air into the water stream. For a household averaging 8-minute showers, this reduces water consumption by up to 40% per shower.
sourceNotes: Waterwise UK guidance on shower water consumption and low-flow showerheads. Energy Saving Trust guidance on water and energy savings from shower efficiency. Thames Water and other utility guidance on free eco showerhead schemes.

content.text: Measuring existing flow rate: place a 10-litre bucket under your showerhead, turn the shower on at normal temperature, and time how long to fill the bucket. 40 seconds or less means you have a high-flow showerhead (15 l/min or above). 60 seconds or more means you are already at a moderate flow rate (10 l/min). An
at 8 l/min would take 75 seconds to fill the same bucket.

are the lowest-cost option (a few pounds) and fit into the existing showerhead. They reduce flow but do not improve the spray quality. An aerated replacement showerhead gives a better result and is available in most fitting sizes. Most UK showerheads connect via a standard 1/2" BSP thread and are straightforward to replace: unscrew the old head (usually by hand or with a cloth-wrapped spanner), check the rubber washer is present, and screw on the new head.

Aerated showerheads perform well at mains pressure (typically 1-3 bar). In low-pressure gravity-fed systems (common in older houses with a cold water tank in the loft), a low-flow showerhead can result in an unsatisfyingly trickle. Check the minimum pressure rating on the showerhead packaging: most eco showerheads specify a minimum of 0.5-1 bar. If you are on a gravity-fed system with poor pressure, a flow restrictor will make the situation worse.

A 4-minute shower timer placed in view makes shower length visible. Some water companies hand them out free; others sell them cheaply online. Research by Waterwise and water companies finds that households cut shower time by 1-2 minutes once they can see how long they have been in there. Combined with an aerated showerhead, a 4-minute target at 8 l/min uses 32 litres. A 10-minute shower at 12 l/min uses 120 litres.
